question.text: What is a 'bull call spread' and what is its payoff profile?

answer.text: Buy a lower strike call and sell a higher strike call with the same expiry — max profit = (high strike - low strike) - net premium paid; max loss = net premium paid; both profit and loss are capped
Buy two calls at different strikes without selling any
Buy a call and simultaneously buy a put at a lower strike
Sell a lower strike call and buy a higher strike call

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bull call spread: long K1 call + short K2 call (K2 > K1). Net premium = premium paid - premium received. Max profit (at S ≥ K2) = (K2-K1) - net premium. Max loss (at S ≤ K1) = net premium. BEP = K1 + net premium. It's a debit spread (you pay net premium). **The Trap:** Selling the lower strike and buying the higher is a bear call spread (credit spread). Buying two calls is a different strategy (ratio spread or simply two directional bets). **Cross-Exam Context:** Bull call spreads work across equity (VIII Ch5), currency (I Ch5), and IR options. The structure is identical regardless of the underlying asset class. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Bull call spreads are the default moderately bullish strategy — they reduce premium cost by sacrificing unlimited upside. Choose strikes based on your expected price target.
